critique.summary: The article introduces Tooscut, a professional video editing application that runs entirely in the browser using WebGPU and WebAssembly (WASM) technologies. It highlights features such as GPU-accelerated rendering, multi-track timeline editing, keyframe animation, and real-time effects without requiring any software installation.
mainPoints: Tooscut is a browser-based non-linear editor (NLE) that leverages WebGPU and Rust/WASM for performance comparable to native applications.
It offers GPU-accelerated compositing for real-time previews and exports.
The editor supports a multi-track timeline with unlimited video and audio tracks, linked clips, and cross-transitions.
Users can animate any property using keyframe animation with bezier easing curves.
Real-time GPU-computed effects such as brightness, contrast, saturation, blur, and hue rotation are available with instant preview.
The application requires no installation and keeps all media local using the File System Access API.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Tooscut's capabilities align with current advancements in web technologies like WebGPU and WASM, which enable high-performance computing in browsers. WebGPU is designed to provide GPU acceleration in web applications, and Rust/WASM is known for efficient performance. The features described, such as multi-track timelines, keyframe animation, and real-time effects, are plausible given these technologies. However, independent reviews or benchmarks of Tooscut specifically are limited, so while the technical claims are credible, the extent of performance parity with native apps would require further verification.
considerations: The article does not provide third-party reviews or user testimonials to substantiate performance claims.
It lacks comparison with other browser-based or native video editing tools to contextualize its advantages or limitations.
Potential browser compatibility issues or hardware requirements for WebGPU are not discussed.
Security and privacy implications of using the File System Access API, while media stays local, are not elaborated.
The article does not mention if there are any limitations on file formats, export options, or project sizes.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around a browser-based professional video editing tool using WebGPU and WASM. Commenters express cautious optimism about its potential, noting current limitations, licensing restrictions, and comparisons to existing tools, while also highlighting technical challenges and feature gaps.

comments.author: TechSquidTV
comments.text: I like the promise, but the hill is very steep and I don't see much on delivery here. Very hopeful, but I would rather see this kind of thing launch significantly further than where it is at. This appears to be a good base, now let's see it again when there is Text support, animations, transitions, filters, etc.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a balanced and realistic assessment of the project's current state and future potential, emphasizing the gap between promise and delivery.

comments.author: caijia
comments.text:
Curious about the headless story. The "Photopea of video editing" comparison is apt — Photopea's real moat ended up being the API and programmatic access, not just the browser UI. Companies embed it for automated image processing workflows.

For a browser-native NLE, the killer feature might not be the timeline UI — it could be exposing the WGPU rendering pipeline as an API that other tools can call. Imagine CI pipelines that render video assets without needing FFmpeg on the server.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully highlights the importance of API access and automation capabilities as a key differentiator for browser-native editing tools.

comments.author: bilekas
comments.text: Nice tool, but not a very useful license.. I would love to integrate something like this as an additive to users but if I'm not mistaken, that's completely off limits for this license type ?
comments.analysis: The comment raises a valid concern about licensing restrictions that could limit integration and wider adoption, which is important for potential users to consider.

comments.author: Retr0id
comments.text: Tried it in Firefox and it was working for a few minutes and then managed to crash the whole browser. Definitely a firefox and/or gpu driver bug though. I can't wait for WebGPU browser/platform support to get a bit more mature, because it's awesome (although the security implications do make me nervous).
comments.analysis: This comment provides a firsthand experience with stability issues, attributing them plausibly to browser or GPU driver immaturity, which aligns with known WebGPU challenges.

comments.author: xrd
comments.text: I've been using kdenlive and it is functional as an open source video editor. I don't know if kdenlive supports shared assets and projects, but this feels like something this project could offer and exceed expectations. Is that on the roadmap?
comments.analysis: The comment offers a useful comparison with an established open source editor and suggests a potential feature direction, though it is speculative about the new project's roadmap.

comments.author: thefourthchime
comments.text: This is very cool!! but a test video I did and I played it back on Safari, the video playback was very, very choppy (m2 air). Is this a known issue?
comments.analysis: This user reports a specific performance issue on Safari with a particular device, which is a valuable practical observation but may be hardware or browser specific.

comments.author: bitwize
comments.text: Is it feature-parity with Adobe Premiere and Final Cut Pro? If not, then it's not professional.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses a rigid viewpoint on professionalism tied strictly to feature parity with major commercial software, which overlooks the value of innovation and niche use cases.

comments.author: gnarbarian
comments.text:
very cool. I was trying to implement a MP4 encoder in webGPU recently by porting sections of ffmpeg (NOT EASY).

it's for this:

https://ubernaut.github.io/recordMyScreen/

which uses a the wasm build of ffmpeg.
comments.analysis: The comment adds technical context about the complexity of implementing video encoding in WebGPU, supporting the challenges faced by the project.

comments.author: Jaxkr
comments.text: Great project. The last time someone did this idea well they got acquired by Microsoft. Clipchamp has since been enshittified, making them ripe for disruption. The wheel continues to turn…
comments.analysis: This comment provides historical context and a critical perspective on a competitor, suggesting market opportunity but with some emotional language.

comments.author: skiing_crawling
comments.text: What would be really awesome is if it could use the server its hosted on's GPUs. I have a multi GPU server and it would be great to be able to edit videos from my table or couch without spinning up my laptop so hard.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a constructive feature suggestion that could enhance usability and performance by leveraging server-side GPU resources.
